behaviors.description: The generated reply references specific points from the incoming email rather than producing a generic response.
behaviors.justification: A mock LLM would always return a pre-configured response, completely hiding whether the real LLM actually understands and responds to specific email details. A real failure could be that the prompt doesn't include the email content, or the LLM ignores it and produces a generic response. Only a real LLM call can verify the model generates contextually relevant output addressing specific claims or requests from the email.
behaviors.test_description: Call draft generation with a real email thread containing 2-3 specific, non-generic factual claims (e.g., 'I noticed your Q4 revenue was down 15%'). Verify the generated reply contains references to those specific points (by keyword or semantic similarity) rather than generic greetings or boilerplate.

behaviors.description: The factual consistency check flags claims in the draft that contradict entries in the knowledge base.
behaviors.justification: Mocking both the KB and the fact-checking logic only tests the conditional glue between them. Real failures include: KB query returns the wrong entry or misses a relevant one, fact-checker fails to recognize a real contradiction, or the integration between fact-checker and KB breaks. These gaps only surface when using real (or realistic) KB entries and real fact-checking logic. (If the fact-checker is LLM-based, escalate to Agentic.)
behaviors.test_description: Create a test KB with specific factual entries (e.g., 'Product X ships in 2-3 weeks'). Generate a draft claiming 'Product X ships next week'. Run the consistency check and verify it flags the contradiction. Also test non-contradictory claims to ensure no false positives.

behaviors.description: When the knowledge base has no relevant entries, the draft includes a disclaimer rather than fabricating facts.
behaviors.justification: This is pure conditional logic: if KB returns no relevant results → add disclaimer. The KB query logic and fact-checking accuracy are tested separately (Behavior 2). The real failure here is simply: code doesn't add the disclaimer when KB is empty. A Unit test with a mock KB returning an empty list will catch this failure.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the KB to return an empty result set. Generate a draft. Verify the output includes a disclaimer statement (e.g., 'I could not find relevant information to verify this claim') and does not contain fabricated facts or unsourced claims.

behaviors.description: The approval workflow blocks sending until the user explicitly approves — no draft is sent automatically.
behaviors.justification: An in-process mock of send() can verify the conditional check exists (Unit), but real failures include approval state loss between steps, race conditions where send() is triggered via another code path, or persistence issues with the approval flag. These are integration concerns that a pure mock cannot catch. The blocking behavior must be verified across module boundaries and state transitions.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the full approval workflow: (1) Generate draft, (2) user declines approval, verify send() is not called; (3) generate new draft, (4) user approves, verify send() is called and the approved draft is delivered. Test both paths to catch state management failures.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — read thread, generate draft, check facts, present for approval — works end-to-end for a real email thread.
behaviors.justification: Multiple sequential agentic steps (LLM generation, possibly LLM-based fact-checking, approval state transitions) with non-determinism and interdependencies that only surface when the full pipeline runs together. Real failure: individual components work in isolation but fail in sequence (e.g., LLM output from draft generation doesn't parse for fact-checking, or approval state breaks the approval UI). Only end-to-end testing with real LLM calls and all components wired together catches these integration failures.
behaviors.test_description: Fetch or provide a real email thread with mixed facts (some verifiable against KB, some requiring a disclaimer). Run the complete pipeline: (1) read/parse email, (2) generate draft with real LLM, (3) run fact-checker, (4) present for approval, (5) user approves. Verify the final sent output is factually consistent, addresses the email, and includes appropriate disclaimers where KB is silent.
